questions.prompt: The CPU instruction cycle consists of which basic phases?
questions.explanation: Basic instruction cycle: Fetch (read instruction from memory), Decode (interpret opcode), Execute (perform operation). Write-back may also be included.

questions.prompt: The Program Counter (PC) register holds:
choices: A. The result of the current arithmetic operation
B. The address of the next instruction to be fetched
D. The current instruction being decoded
questions.explanation: PC (Program Counter): holds the address of the next instruction. Automatically incremented after each fetch; modified by jumps/branches.

questions.prompt: Immediate addressing mode means the operand is:
choices: B. Encoded directly in the instruction itself
C. At the address contained in a register
questions.explanation: Immediate: operand = literal value in the instruction (e.g., ADD R0, #5 → adds 5 directly). No memory access needed for operand.

questions.prompt: Register indirect addressing mode means the operand is:
choices: A. A literal in the instruction
C. At the memory address stored in the specified register
D. At an absolute fixed address
questions.explanation: Register indirect: the register holds a pointer to the operand's memory address. E.g., LDR R1, [R0] → load from address in R0.

questions.prompt: The difference between RISC and CISC architectures is primarily:
choices: A. RISC: complex instructions in hardware; CISC: simple instructions only
B. RISC: simple fixed-length instructions, load-store architecture; CISC: complex multi-cycle instructions
C. RISC uses more memory; CISC uses less
D. CISC uses pipelining; RISC does not
questions.explanation: RISC: simple, fixed-length instructions, many registers, load/store memory model (ARM, MIPS). CISC: complex instructions, variable length (x86). RISC easier to pipeline.

questions.prompt: Pipelining in a CPU increases performance by:
choices: A. Running one instruction at a time faster
B. Overlapping execution of multiple instructions across pipeline stages
questions.explanation: Pipeline: multiple instructions in different stages simultaneously (fetch stage 1 while decode stage 2 while execute stage 3). Ideal throughput: 1 instruction per clock cycle.

questions.prompt: A pipeline hazard (stall) occurs when:
choices: A. The CPU runs too fast
B. A later instruction depends on the result of an earlier one not yet completed (data hazard), or a branch changes the flow (control hazard)
questions.explanation: Data hazard: read-after-write dependency → stall. Control hazard: branch target unknown → stall or speculative execution. Structural hazard: resource conflict.

questions.prompt: Cache memory improves CPU performance by:
choices: B. Storing frequently-used data/instructions in fast memory close to the CPU
questions.explanation: Cache: small, fast SRAM between CPU and main DRAM. Exploits locality of reference. L1 (fastest/smallest) → L2 → L3 → main memory hierarchy.

questions.prompt: The stack data structure used by CPUs operates as:
choices: B. Last-in, first-out (LIFO) — PUSH adds to top, POP removes from top
D. First-in, last-out only for subroutines
questions.explanation: Stack: LIFO. PUSH decrements SP, stores data. POP loads data, increments SP. Used for subroutine return addresses and local variables.

questions.prompt: An interrupt service routine (ISR) is invoked when:
choices: A. The program executes a JUMP instruction
B. A hardware or software event triggers an interrupt, pausing normal execution
questions.explanation: Interrupt: hardware/software event → CPU saves PC and status, jumps to ISR → services interrupt → restores context → resumes normal code.

questions.prompt: In a memory-mapped I/O scheme, peripheral devices are accessed using:
choices: B. The same address space as memory — ordinary load/store instructions
questions.explanation: Memory-mapped I/O: peripherals appear at specific memory addresses → CPU uses standard MOV/LDR/STR to access them (ARM, most modern CPUs).

questions.prompt: DMA (Direct Memory Access) is used to:
choices: A. Allow peripherals to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU involvement
questions.explanation: DMA: DMA controller handles data transfers (e.g., ADC → RAM, disk → RAM) freeing CPU for other tasks. CPU sets up transfer then yields bus.

questions.prompt: The 2's complement representation of −5 in 8-bit binary is:
A 16-bit address bus can directly address how many unique memory locations?
questions.explanation: 2¹⁶ = 65,536 locations = 64 K addresses. An 8-bit data bus means 64 KB addressable memory.

questions.prompt: The function of the ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) is:
choices: B. Perform arithmetic (add, subtract) and logical (AND, OR, XOR) operations
questions.explanation: ALU: performs integer arithmetic and bitwise logic. Combined with FPU for floating-point. Core component of every CPU.

questions.prompt: Which types of RAM are volatile (lose data when power is removed)? (Select ALL that apply.)
questions.explanation: SRAM and DRAM: volatile. Flash and EEPROM: non-volatile (retain data without power). DRAM needs refresh; SRAM does not.

questions.prompt: A subroutine call typically involves:
choices: A. Saving the return address on the stack, then jumping to the subroutine
questions.explanation: CALL: push return address (PC+1) onto stack → jump to subroutine. RET: pop return address → resume. Stack-based call mechanism.

questions.prompt: Big-endian vs. little-endian refers to:
choices: B. Byte order in multi-byte values — big-endian: MSB at lowest address; little-endian: LSB at lowest address
questions.explanation: Big-endian (Motorola, network byte order): MSB first. Little-endian (Intel x86, ARM default): LSB at lowest address. Important for data interoperability.

questions.prompt: The purpose of an instruction register (IR) is to:
choices: B. Hold the currently-fetched instruction while it is being decoded
questions.explanation: IR: holds the fetched instruction for decoding. The control unit reads the IR to determine what operation to perform.

questions.prompt: Indexed addressing mode computes the effective address as:
questions.explanation: Indexed: EA = base_reg + displacement. Useful for array access: base = start of array, offset = index × element_size.

questions.prompt: A branch prediction unit in a CPU is designed to:
choices: B. Reduce pipeline stalls by guessing the outcome of conditional branches
questions.explanation: Branch predictor: guesses taken/not-taken for conditional branches → pre-loads predicted path → avoids stall if prediction correct. Misprediction penalty = pipeline flush.

questions.prompt: What is the hexadecimal representation of the decimal number 255?
Virtual memory allows a process to:
choices: A. Access more address space than physical RAM by using disk as backing store
D. Use ROM as a working memory
questions.explanation: Virtual memory: OS maps virtual addresses to physical pages; pages not in RAM stored on disk (page fault → swap in). Enables process isolation and large address spaces.

questions.prompt: Amdahl's law implies that overall speedup is limited by:
choices: B. The fraction of program execution time that cannot be improved or parallelized
D. Number of instructions in the ISA
questions.explanation: Amdahl: Speedup = 1/((1-p)+p/s). Even infinite speedup on fraction p leaves the serial fraction (1-p) as the bottleneck.

questions.prompt: In a direct-mapped cache, each main-memory block maps to:
choices: B. Exactly one cache line determined by index bits
D. A line selected by replacement policy only
questions.explanation: Direct-mapped cache has one possible location per memory block. Fast and simple but prone to conflict misses.
